overview.description: To provide navigation for paginated content.
overview.purpose: To provide navigation for paginated content.
whenToUse: When you need to navigate through a set of paginated content.
whenNotToUse: When you have a single page of content or don't need pagination.

events.name: pagesizechange
events.description: Emitted when the rows per page value is changed.

events.name: pagechange
events.description: Emitted when the page is changed.

slots.description: The default slot for the router links

members.name: labels
members.description:
Labels object for customizing component text.
If not provided, labels are automatically loaded based on document's lang attribute.

members.name: totalpages
members.description: Number of total pages.

members.name: totalrecords
members.description:
Total number of rows across all pages.
Required when `showpagestatus` is enabled to display accurate row count (e.g., "1-10 of 75 rows").
If not provided when `showpagestatus` is true, the status will show a simplified format without the total (e.g., "1-10").

members.name: currentpage
members.description: Number of the selected page.

members.name: hiddenlabels
members.description:
If set to `true` or the attribute is just presented without any value,
then the `previous` and `next` label will not appear,
but will be used as `aria-label` only

members.name: showlabels
members.description:
If set to `true` or the attribute is just presented without any value,
then the `previous`, `next`, `first`, and `last` labels will appear,
By default, labels for those buttons are hidden but are used as `aria-label`
@default false

members.name: visiblepages
members.description:
Maximum number of visible pages to be displayed.
If the `disabledtruncation` is set to `false` the dots used to truncate the pages will also be counted.
When set to `0` only navigation buttons will be displayed.

members.name: previouslabel
members.description:
Label for the `previous` button.
The value will also be used as aria-label on the button.

members.name: nextlabel
members.description:
Label for the `next` button.
The value will also be used as aria-label on the button.

members.name: arialabel
members.description: The `aria-label` for the pagination wrapper

members.name: fit
members.description: Fit of the pagination.

members.name: disabledtruncation
members.description:
If set to `true` or the attribute is just presented without any value,
then three dots will not be used to truncate the pages and first and last page navigation will not be visible.

members.name: variant
members.description:
Variant of the pagination component.
- 'button': Standard pagination with numbered page buttons (1, 2, 3...), recommended to use for content pagination (e.g., search results and articles)
- 'select': Advanced pagination with page selector dropdown instead of numbered buttons, recommended to use for tables with less than 300 rows
- 'input': Minimal pagination with only navigation arrows (first/prev/next/last), recommended to use for tables with more than 300 rows

members.name: showfirstandlastpage
members.description: If set to `true`, displays first and last navigation buttons (double arrows) in addition to prev/next buttons.

members.name: showpagesize
members.description:
If set to `true`, displays a rows per page selector dropdown in the start section.
Can be combined with any variant.

members.name: showpagestatus
members.description:
If set to `true`, displays a status text showing the current range of items in the start section.
When `totalrecords` is provided: shows "1-10 of 75 rows"
When `totalrecords` is not provided: shows simplified format "1-10"
Can be combined with any variant.

members.name: pagesizeoptions
members.description: Data for the rows per page selector dropdown options as an array of numbers.

members.name: pagesize
members.description: Currently selected rows per page value.

members.name: pagechange
members.description: Page number changed

members.name: pagesizechange
members.description: Rows per page value changed

types.name: IMcPaginationLabels

members.name: previous
members.description: aria-label (or label when showlabel is on) for Previous page button

members.name: next
members.description: aria-label (or label when showlabel is on) for Next page button

members.name: first
members.description: aria-label (or label when showlabel is on) for First page button

members.name: last
members.description: aria-label (or label when showlabel is on) for Last page button

members.name: ariaEllipsis
members.description: aria-label for ellipsis

members.name: ariaCurrentPage
members.description: aria-label for current page number button

members.name: ariaPage
members.description: aria-label for page number buttons

members.name: ariaSelectLabel
members.description: hidden label for mc-select in select variant

members.name: ariaInputLabel
members.description: hidden label for mc-input in input variant

types.jsdoc:
Labels configuration for mc-pagination component
Extends common labels with pagination specific labels

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container element inside the mc-pagination component where the main page navigation is located (buttons, select or input). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: navigation

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container where page size select is located (if enabled). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: pagesize

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container where page status text is located (if enabled). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: pagestatus

guidelines.title: Pagination
guidelines.description: Pagination is used to split large amounts of content (such as search results or table rows) into manageable chunks across multiple pages.

best-practice: Only split up items across pages when there are more than 25
Show no more than 5 pages on narrow screens (mobile) and 10 pages on wide screens (desktop)
Place the pagination control directly below the page content it controls
truncation: The pagination uses truncation by default to allow the user to navigate to either the first or the last page and to see the total number of pages regardless of which page is currently active. In certain cases when truncation may not be desirable - this could be the case for search results where the total number of pages is often irrelevant - it can be disabled.
simplification: In cases where the user needs for pagination are very light or if the screen real estate is limited it is possible to hide the button labels (by enabling the ‘hiddenlabels' control) and/or the page numbers (by setting the 'visiblepages’ control to 0).
pagination-alternatives: This component is general-purpose pagination. However, in some cases more simple or advanced controls may be appropriate.
infinite-scroll: Allows the user to seamlessly scroll down a long list of items without overloading the server. This approach is generally discouraged due to the lack of user control and an inability to reach the bottom of the page.
'load-more'-button: Allows the user to load more results on demand without having to reload the entire page or dealing with a pagination control (which has been shown to discourage users from exploring past the first page). This approach is encouraged when the user is browsing through items (explorative) rather than looking for a specific/known item (goal-oriented)
advanced-pagination: See the total number of items
See the number of items displayed on each page
See the interval of items displayed on the active page
Change the number of items displayed on each page
Manually enter and navigate to any specific page

designPrinciples.title: Spacing
designPrinciples.description: Consistent spacing creates groups, rhythm and good scan-ability.

use-4-pixel-spacing: The base unit for spacing is 4 pixels, subsequent units are multiples of 4 (such as 8, 12, 16, etc.). These units should be used consistently for all paddings, margins, gutters, typography, and layouts to ensure a cohesive appearance and user experience across all Maersk digital products.
exceptions-to-4-pixel-spacing: When creating components, finer control is needed for spacing elements. Three acceptable exceptions here are 2, 6, and 10 pixels.
components: Relate the component elements and still keep them distinct by using space units dervided from the 4-pixel base spacing. These space units will make aligning and using non-core components with core ones easier.
patterns: A recognisable pattern is created when consistent spacing is used to establish a visual rhythm and relationship between the pattern elements.

Pattern spacing needs to be coherent which means not all space units need to be the same. Several spacing units can help establish better visual balance when dealing with differently-sized UI elements in the pattern.
layouts: Visual hierarchy in layouts requires well spaced regions and UI elements. Spacing units can control the information density of any application layout. Use them to provide the right level for the context and users' expectation.
information-density: Space is the element that controls the information density of any interface. Adjusting the space between blocks on information creates a visual rhythm and hierarchy.

High information density can be disorienting and overwhelming if spacing does not allow the eyes to rest and let people process what they see. People may notice the grouped elements but not process each individual item.

We recommend reading the <PageLink path="/design-language/component-fit/">Fit guidelines</PageLink> to help you make an informed decision what spacing units will be most suitable for your layout composition.

relatedGuidelines.title: Accessibility
relatedGuidelines.description: Improving the user experience for everyone.

what-is-accessibility?: Accessibility means that websites, tools and technologies are designed and developed in a way that makes it possible for people with disabilities to use them. Accessibility also benefits people without disabilities for example people with temporary disabilities or situational disabilities.
Accessibility helps people perceive, understand and interact with our digital products and it takes auditory, cognitive, neurological, physical, speech and visual limitations into consideration.

But accessibility is also a mindset and a change in the way we think and talk about digital products. It is a cultural change that cannot be made by one team only. We, therefore, urge all our colleagues as well as top management to get involved and share awareness about this very important topic. We need your support!
why-is-accessibility-important?: One of the main myths about accessibility is that it only concerns a small group of people with severe disabilities. This assumption is far from the reality and according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), more than 1 billion people are living with some level of impairment that affects the way they interact with digital products and access information online.
When our websites, apps and other digital products are designed badly, they create barriers that exclude people and make access to our offerings cumbersome and in some cases not possible at all.
being-inaccessible-is-a-problem: It is an ethical problem when we exclude certain groups of people from our products and services.

It is a legal problem, especially in the US where companies are being sued if they are not accessible, which could have financial consequences in terms of fines as well as affecting the brand negatively.

It is a big business problem - if we choose to exclude a very big group of potential customers because they have a disability.
When we design and build products with accessibility in mind, we not only open up our products and experiences for more people, but our products start to become more human - in the sense that they reflect the diversity of society and become much more flexible and robust to accommodate many different needs and use cases.
